posts.title: A Research Program for Inspectable ML
posts.description: An essay on reading foundational deep learning papers as one research program and turning interpretability and representation analysis into a rigorous science.
posts.socialPreview: Why the next phase of deep learning depends on making interpretability and representation analysis rigorous, not just scaling models.

faq.question: Is this just about scaling models?
faq.answer: No. The essay argues that progress comes from organizing computation around the structure of a task, then making learned representations inspectable enough to evaluate and trust.

faq.question: What kind of interpretability does this argue for?
faq.answer: It favors frozen-model tools such as probing, representation analysis, attribution, and information-theoretic views that can become standard evaluation interfaces around trained systems.

posts.title: The mental models of deep learning
posts.description: AI research explained through foundational deep learning papers: universal approximation, convolutional architectures, transformers, reinforcement learning, and the open question of task-fit architecture.
posts.socialPreview: A paper-backed AI research essay connecting Cybenko, LeCun, Vaswani, Mnih, architecture design, representation learning, and the limits of today's theory.

faq.question: Is deep learning research only about bigger models?
faq.answer: No. The essay treats scale as one axis, but the main question is how architectures, objectives, data, and training loops make a model fit the structure of a task.

faq.question: Why include reinforcement learning beside architecture papers?
faq.answer: Deep RL is part of the same AI research question because it asks how a trainable system can discover useful behavior from delayed, noisy feedback instead of only labeled examples.

faq.question: What gap does this reading trail leave open?
faq.answer: It leaves open a theory of when a particular architecture, training objective, or memory mechanism is the right fit for a task rather than merely an empirically successful choice.

posts.title: An Engineering Discipline for Agentic Coding
posts.description: A project write-up on treating agent-assisted development as a verifiable engineering system with operating rules, skills, checks, and review boundaries.
posts.socialPreview: Agentic coding works best when stochastic generation is surrounded by deterministic checks, clear boundaries, and human accountability.

faq.question: Is AgentCoding just a prompt collection?
faq.answer: No. The write-up frames AgentCoding as an operating system for agent-assisted development: canonical instructions, task routing, deterministic gates, security policy, and final reporting.

faq.question: What problem does it solve?
faq.answer: It targets the reliability problem created when agents can generate code faster than humans can inspect it, using repository rules and checks to keep behavior bounded and reviewable.

posts.title: Forecasting the Limit Order Book
posts.description: An applied ML write-up on mid-price movement forecasting with logistic regression, MLP, DeepLOB-style sequence modeling, and interpretability probes.
posts.socialPreview: A candid applied-ML case study on noisy limit order book forecasting, strong baselines, sequence models, and what the current metrics do and do not prove.

faq.question: Does this claim to be a production trading model?
faq.answer: No. The write-up presents exploratory notebook results and explicitly separates baseline metrics, validation signals, and reproducibility limitations from production claims.

faq.question: Why include interpretability probes?
faq.answer: Because noisy financial data can reward artifacts. Feature ablation, saliency, and integrated gradients help test whether model behavior is tied to plausible order-book structure.

posts.title: Why AGENTS.md Wasn't Enough for My Repo
posts.description: A first-person account of why scattered instruction files failed me and why a repo-owned boundary became the real problem to solve.
posts.socialPreview: I had a PR slip through conflicting instruction files, which pushed me to build a stricter repo-level system for agent workflows.

posts.title: How to Win a Hackathon
posts.description: A practical essay on why hackathons are won by matching the idea, demo, and technical work to the organizers and judging criteria.
posts.socialPreview: Hackathons are not won by technical sophistication alone. They are won by understanding the requirements, the judges, and the idea they are ready to reward.

posts.title: What Beryl Actually Does (and What It Deliberately Doesn't)
posts.description: A practical walkthrough of the first working structure: `.beryl/agent/`, a deterministic check, and review evidence in files.
posts.socialPreview: The difference was moving checks from prompts to versioned repository files, then proving the behavior through deterministic checks.

posts.title: Week 1 of Building Beryl in Public: What People Told Me
posts.description: What the first feedback cycle exposed in week one: strictness tradeoffs, false positives, and direction for future iterations.
posts.socialPreview: The check surfaced real mismatch cases, and this week shaped the first iteration around realistic review boundaries and feedback loops.

posts.title: NP-Completeness: Formal Definition, Proof Sketches, and Reductions
posts.description: NP-completeness explained through formal definitions, proof sketches, reductions, and why hardness evidence changes algorithm design.
posts.socialPreview: A proof-backed guide to NP-completeness: formal definition, reductions, Cook, Karp, and the design consequences of hardness evidence.

faq.question: Does NP-complete mean impossible to solve quickly?
faq.answer: No. NP-complete means that a polynomial-time exact algorithm for this problem would imply polynomial-time algorithms for every problem in NP. It is strong evidence to change the design target, not a proof that every useful instance is hopeless.

faq.question: Why do NP-completeness proofs use decision problems?
faq.answer: The definitions of P, NP, and NP-completeness are stated for languages with yes/no answers. Optimization problems usually enter the framework through threshold versions, such as asking whether there is a tour with cost at most K.

faq.question: What is the most common weak point in a reduction proof?
faq.answer: The reverse direction. It is not enough to show that a satisfying source solution creates a target solution. You also need to show that every target solution decodes into a valid source solution.
